sections.plain_summary: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) occurs when bacteria that normally live in the large intestine migrate into or overpopulate the small intestine, where they don't belong. These bacteria ferment food as it passes through, producing gases and byproducts that cause bloating, pain, altered bowel habits, and — in more severe cases — nutrient malabsorption. It is a structural problem with a microbial driver, and it is one of the most common undiagnosed explanations for persistent gut symptoms that have been labelled IBS.
sections.detailed_summary:
The small intestine is designed to absorb nutrients, not to host large communities of bacteria. Normally, a combination of stomach acid, bile, digestive enzymes, and a muscular wave called the migrating motor complex (MMC) keeps bacterial populations in check. When any of these protective mechanisms fail — due to motility problems, low stomach acid, structural abnormalities, prior infection, or immune dysfunction — bacteria can colonise the small intestine in excessive numbers.

Once established, these bacteria intercept carbohydrates before they can be absorbed, fermenting them and producing hydrogen, methane, or hydrogen sulphide gas. This fermentation happens much earlier in the digestive tract than it should, causing rapid, pronounced bloating often within 60–90 minutes of eating. The bacterial load can also damage the intestinal lining, disrupt the brush border enzymes responsible for digesting sugars and absorbing nutrients, and trigger immune and inflammatory responses.

SIBO is not a single entity. Different populations of organisms produce different gases, and these subtypes have meaningfully different symptom profiles, treatment responses, and prognoses. Hydrogen-dominant SIBO tends to present with diarrhoea or mixed bowel habits; methane-dominant overgrowth (now more precisely called intestinal methanogen overgrowth, or IMO) tends to produce constipation and slower transit; hydrogen sulphide SIBO may cause diarrhoea with a particularly foul smell and is harder to detect on standard breath tests.

The condition is strongly linked to IBS — research suggests SIBO may be present in 30–85% of IBS cases depending on diagnostic criteria used — and is also associated with conditions that slow gut motility or alter digestive chemistry, including hypothyroidism, diabetes, connective tissue disorders like hEDS, use of proton pump inhibitors, and prior abdominal surgery. Post-infectious SIBO, triggered by food poisoning, is now well-documented: certain pathogens trigger an autoimmune response that damages the nerves governing the MMC, leaving the small intestine susceptible to bacterial colonisation.

Diagnosis is most commonly made via lactulose or glucose breath testing, which measures exhaled gases produced by bacterial fermentation. These tests have real limitations — false negatives are common, and interpretation criteria vary between labs — but they remain the most accessible non-invasive option. Small intestinal aspirate and culture is the technical gold standard but is invasive and rarely performed in routine care.

Treatment typically involves antibiotics (most commonly rifaximin, alone or combined with neomycin for methane-dominant cases), elemental diets, or herbal antimicrobial protocols. However, treating SIBO without addressing the underlying driver — whatever allowed bacteria to accumulate in the first place — leads to frequent relapse. Long-term management focuses on motility support, addressing upstream causes, and maintaining a gut environment that prevents re-colonisation.

prevalence.summary: Common but widely underdiagnosed, particularly among people with chronic IBS-like symptoms.
prevalence.estimate: Estimated in 6–15% of healthy adults; 30–85% of those diagnosed with IBS depending on criteria used
prevalence.notes: Prevalence figures vary dramatically because diagnostic criteria and breath test thresholds differ between studies and institutions. Some researchers argue the true rate is higher due to under-testing. SIBO appears more common in women, older adults, people with motility disorders, those who use PPIs long-term, and those with connective tissue disorders. Post-infectious SIBO is increasingly recognised as a distinct and significant subgroup.

subgroups.name: Hydrogen SIBO (H2-SIBO)
subgroups.description: The most commonly identified SIBO subtype, driven by hydrogen-producing bacteria (primarily gram-negative facultative anaerobes) fermenting carbohydrates in the small intestine. The excess hydrogen is absorbed into the bloodstream and exhaled, where it can be detected by breath testing.
distinguishing_features: Elevated hydrogen on lactulose or glucose breath test (≥20 ppm rise within 90 minutes)
More commonly associated with diarrhoea-predominant or mixed bowel habits
Bloating and gas typically worse after carbohydrate-rich meals
May cause more pronounced brush border enzyme damage, leading to secondary lactose and fructose intolerance

subgroups.name: Intestinal methanogen overgrowth (IMO) / Methane SIBO
subgroups.description: Caused by archaea (primarily Methanobrevibacter smithii) that consume hydrogen produced by other microbes and release methane. Methane slows intestinal transit, producing a distinct constipation-dominant picture. Now often referred to as intestinal methanogen overgrowth (IMO) because the organisms are archaea rather than bacteria.
distinguishing_features: Elevated methane on breath test (≥10 ppm at any point is considered positive by many protocols)
Strongly associated with constipation — methane gas directly inhibits intestinal smooth muscle contraction
Bloating that is often diffuse and persistent rather than meal-triggered
Frequently more treatment-resistant; often requires combination antibiotic protocols (e.g., rifaximin plus neomycin or metronidazole)
Can co-occur with hydrogen SIBO (mixed pattern)

subgroups.name: Hydrogen sulphide SIBO (H2S-SIBO)
subgroups.description: Driven by sulphur-reducing bacteria that produce hydrogen sulphide gas. H2S-SIBO was historically difficult to detect because standard breath tests do not measure hydrogen sulphide; newer tri-gas testing is now available but not yet widely accessible.
distinguishing_features: May produce a 'flat-line' pattern on standard hydrogen/methane breath tests (sometimes called 'negative SIBO' despite ongoing symptoms)
Often associated with diarrhoea, urgency, and a distinctive sulphurous or 'rotten egg' odour to gas
High-sulphur foods (eggs, meat, cruciferous vegetables, alliums) may worsen symptoms
Hydrogen sulphide at elevated levels may have toxic effects on colonocytes and contribute to intestinal permeability
Requires tri-gas breath testing for identification; not all labs offer this

symptoms.notes: Characteristically develops within 60–90 minutes of eating, particularly after carbohydrates or fermentable foods. Can cause visible abdominal distension. Many people describe looking 'pregnant' by afternoon.

symptoms.symptom: Excessive gas and belching or flatulence
symptoms.notes: Gas production occurs earlier in the digestive tract than normal, leading to both upper (belching) and lower (flatulence) symptoms.
Pain is often diffuse and crampy rather than localised. Can be triggered or worsened by eating.
More common in hydrogen-dominant and hydrogen sulphide subtypes. Stools may be loose, urgent, or frothy due to fat malabsorption.
More characteristic of methane-dominant SIBO/IMO. Methane gas directly slows intestinal transit.
Common in mixed-type presentations or when both hydrogen and methane are elevated.
Can occur after meals, particularly when SIBO is severe or associated with motility problems.
Indicates fat malabsorption. Stools may float, appear greasy, or be difficult to flush. Occurs in more advanced or severe SIBO.
Particularly vitamin B12 (bacteria consume it), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), iron, and magnesium. These deficiencies can cause their own symptom cascades.
May result from nutrient malabsorption, systemic inflammation from bacterial products (LPS), or disrupted sleep from GI symptoms.
Commonly reported. Proposed mechanisms include systemic absorption of bacterial metabolites, nutritional deficiencies (particularly B12), and gut-brain axis dysregulation.

symptoms.symptom: Food intolerances — especially to FODMAP foods
symptoms.notes: Many people with SIBO develop intolerances to fermentable carbohydrates including onion, garlic, wheat, legumes, lactose, and fructose. These intolerances may be secondary to the overgrowth rather than primary.
Gas pressure from fermentation in the small intestine may contribute to reflux. There is bidirectional overlap — PPI use for reflux can worsen SIBO by reducing stomach acid.
A well-documented association exists between SIBO and rosacea. Systemic immune activation from bacterial products may drive cutaneous inflammation.
Systemic inflammation from SIBO-associated bacterial products may contribute to diffuse joint aching in some individuals.

symptoms.symptom: Mood disturbance — anxiety or low mood
symptoms.notes: Via gut-brain axis signalling and potential effects of microbial metabolites on neurotransmitter production.
Significant malabsorption can cause unintended weight loss. More typical of severe or longstanding SIBO.
An association with SIBO has been noted in some research, possibly mediated by iron or B12 deficiency from malabsorption.

key_patterns: Severe, rapid-onset bloating (within 1–2 hours of eating) that is worse with carbohydrates, fibre, or fermentable foods
IBS diagnosis that has not responded to standard dietary interventions or that worsens on a high-fibre diet
Onset of IBS-like symptoms following a documented episode of food poisoning or gastroenteritis
Multiple secondary food intolerances developing over time (FODMAPs, lactose, fructose) suggesting a common upstream driver
Low B12, iron, or fat-soluble vitamins despite adequate dietary intake
Concurrent motility disorder, hypothyroidism, connective tissue disorder, or long-term PPI use
Flat-line breath test (very low hydrogen AND methane) with diarrhoea and sulphurous gas — possible H2S SIBO
Positive anti-CdtB or anti-vinculin antibodies alongside post-infectious IBS-D pattern

at_home.name: Food and symptom diary with timing
at_home.description: Recording meals alongside symptom onset time can help identify the characteristic SIBO pattern: symptoms beginning 60–90 minutes after eating, worsened by fermentable foods (onion, garlic, legumes, wheat, lactose, fructose, apple). A pattern of symptoms correlating specifically with carbohydrate-containing meals rather than fats or proteins is suggestive.
at_home.safety_notes: This is an observational tool only. Eliminating entire food groups based on observations alone is not recommended long-term without guidance from a dietitian.

at_home.name: Low-FODMAP trial response observation
at_home.description: Some people observe significant symptom reduction when reducing fermentable carbohydrates (FODMAPs). While this does not confirm SIBO — FODMAP sensitivity has many causes — a strong positive response supports the idea that fermentation is driving symptoms and warrants further investigation.
at_home.safety_notes: A low-FODMAP diet is intended as a short-term diagnostic and management tool (typically 2–6 weeks), not a permanent diet. Long-term restriction can adversely affect the microbiome. Work with a FODMAP-trained dietitian where possible.

at_home.name: At-home lactulose or glucose breath test (mail-in kit)
at_home.description: Several companies offer postal breath test kits that can be completed at home after a preparatory diet. The person consumes a lactulose or glucose solution and collects breath samples at timed intervals (usually over 2–3 hours). Samples are mailed to a lab that analyses for hydrogen and methane gas. This mirrors what is done in clinical settings.
at_home.safety_notes: Preparation is critical — failing to follow the pre-test dietary and fasting instructions will produce unreliable results. These tests have significant false-negative rates; a negative result does not rule out SIBO. Interpretation should ideally be done by a practitioner familiar with SIBO breath test patterns.

clinical.name: Lactulose breath test (LBT)
clinical.description: The person consumes a lactulose solution after fasting. Breath is collected every 15–20 minutes for 2–3 hours and analysed for exhaled hydrogen and methane. Elevated early peaks (before colonic arrival of the substrate) suggest small intestinal fermentation. Widely used but has notable false-positive and false-negative rates. Methodology and interpretation criteria vary significantly between labs.
clinical.availability: Available through many gastroenterology departments and private GI clinics. Also available via mail-in kits from specialist labs (e.g. Genova Diagnostics, Aerodiagnostics, QuinTron-based labs).

clinical.name: Glucose breath test (GBT)
clinical.description: Similar to the lactulose breath test but uses glucose as the substrate. Glucose is nearly fully absorbed in the proximal small intestine, so it detects overgrowth in the upper small bowel with higher specificity than lactulose. However, it may miss more distal SIBO. Some clinicians consider it more reliable than LBT for confirming true SIBO.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterology departments and private GI labs. Less commonly offered than lactulose breath testing in some regions.

clinical.name: Hydrogen sulphide breath test
clinical.description: A newer breath test measuring exhaled H2S alongside hydrogen and methane. Developed to address the 'flat-line' breath test pattern seen in suspected H2S SIBO. Currently available through select specialist labs.
clinical.availability: Limited availability. QuinTron and a small number of research-associated labs offer this. Not yet standard of care. Practitioners specialising in SIBO are more likely to know how to access and interpret this test.

clinical.name: Small intestinal aspirate and culture
clinical.description: An endoscope is passed into the small intestine and fluid is aspirated, then cultured to quantify and identify bacterial species. A count of >10³ CFU/mL in the proximal small intestine was the traditional threshold, though this is debated. Technically the gold standard but rarely performed in clinical practice due to invasiveness, cost, and sampling limitations (only samples proximal small bowel).
clinical.availability: Performed in specialist GI centres, usually in the context of research or complex cases. Not routinely available and unlikely to be offered as a first-line investigation.

clinical.name: Anti-CdtB and anti-vinculin antibody test (IBScheck / ibs-smart)
clinical.description: A blood test measuring antibodies associated with post-infectious SIBO. Cytolethal distending toxin B (CdtB) is produced by pathogens such as Campylobacter that can trigger food poisoning. The body's antibody response to CdtB can cross-react with vinculin, a protein in enteric nerve cells, potentially damaging the MMC. Elevated antibodies support a post-infectious mechanism and may help stratify treatment.
clinical.availability: Available through some functional medicine practitioners and specialist GI clinics. Not widely ordered in mainstream gastroenterology. The ibs-smart panel is commercially available in the US and can sometimes be accessed through integrative medicine providers.

clinical.name: Comprehensive stool analysis
clinical.description: Advanced stool testing (e.g. GI-MAP, Genova GI Effects) can identify dysbiosis patterns, markers of inflammation (calprotectin, lactoferrin), pancreatic elastase (to rule out EPI), and the presence of pathogens. Does not directly diagnose SIBO — which is a small intestinal issue — but can help build a picture of the overall gut environment and identify co-existing issues.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine practitioners and some integrative GI specialists. Not standard NHS/Medicare care. Ordered through specialist labs.

clinical.name: Nutrient deficiency panel
clinical.description: Blood tests for B12, folate, iron/ferritin, vitamin D, zinc, and fat-soluble vitamins (A, E, K). Deficiencies — particularly low B12 alongside normal or elevated folate (bacteria produce folate but consume B12) — can support the diagnosis and guide supplementation.
clinical.availability: Orderable by any GP or primary care physician, or privately. Specific patterns (e.g. low B12 with normal/high folate) are worth discussing with a practitioner familiar with SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO and IBS share nearly identical symptom profiles and SIBO is estimated to be present in a large proportion of IBS diagnoses. IBS is a symptom-based label; SIBO is a mechanistic explanation. Many people diagnosed with IBS may have undetected SIBO as an underlying driver.

related_conditions.name: Hydrogen SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Hydrogen SIBO is one of the primary subtypes of SIBO, characterised by hydrogen gas overproduction and typically presenting with diarrhoea or mixed bowel habits.

related_conditions.name: Methane SIBO / Intestinal Methanogen Overgrowth (IMO)
related_conditions.explanation: The methane-dominant subtype of SIBO/IMO is characterised by elevated methane production from archaeal overgrowth, constipation, and slowed transit.

related_conditions.name: Hydrogen sulphide SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: The hydrogen sulphide subtype of SIBO produces H2S gas, causing sulphurous odour, diarrhoea, and often a flat-line breath test result.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Delayed gastric emptying allows food to stagnate and bacterial populations to grow. Gastroparesis is both a risk factor for SIBO and a condition that must be managed to prevent SIBO relapse.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO represents a specific form of microbial dysbiosis — bacterial overgrowth in the wrong location. The two conditions often co-exist and may share common drivers.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal fungal overgrowth (SIFO) / Candida overgrowth
related_conditions.explanation: SIFO can co-exist with SIBO or occur in isolation. Both involve overgrowth of microorganisms in the small intestine and can present with similar symptoms. Some researchers estimate SIFO is present in a significant proportion of unexplained GI symptom cases.

related_conditions.name: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI)
related_conditions.explanation: Both SIBO and EPI cause bloating, fatty stools, and fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies. EPI can also be a risk factor for SIBO because reduced digestive enzyme activity leaves more undigested substrate available for bacterial fermentation.

related_conditions.name: Hypermobile EDS / Hypermobility Spectrum Disorder (hEDS/HSD)
related_conditions.explanation: Connective tissue laxity in hEDS can affect gut motility and the structural integrity of the intestinal wall, making SIBO significantly more common in this population.

related_conditions.name: POTS / Dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Autonomic dysfunction can impair gut motility, particularly the migrating motor complex, contributing to SIBO development. POTS and SIBO frequently co-occur, especially in the hEDS/POTS/MCAS triad.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO-related bacterial products and intestinal permeability changes can trigger mast cell activation. MCAS and SIBO frequently co-exist, particularly in complex multi-system presentations, and can amplify each other's symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Some bacteria in SIBO produce histamine as a metabolic byproduct. Histamine intolerance symptoms — flushing, headaches, hives, nasal congestion after eating — can be secondary to or worsened by SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease can cause intestinal damage and motility changes that predispose to SIBO. Some people with coeliac disease who remain symptomatic on a gluten-free diet may have co-existing SIBO.

related_conditions.name: IBD in remission (Crohn's disease / ulcerative colitis)
related_conditions.explanation: Structural changes from IBD (strictures, surgical resections, motility impairment) increase SIBO risk. SIBO can mimic IBD flares in people with established IBD who are otherwise in remission.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction (hypothyroidism)
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism slows gut motility throughout the GI tract, including the MMC, increasing susceptibility to SIBO. SIBO is significantly more prevalent in hypothyroid individuals.

related_conditions.name: Lactase deficiency
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO can damage the brush border of the small intestine, reducing lactase enzyme activity and causing secondary lactose intolerance. This intolerance may resolve after successful SIBO treatment.

related_conditions.name: Fructose malabsorption
related_conditions.explanation: Similar to secondary lactase deficiency, intestinal damage from SIBO can impair fructose transport. What appears to be primary fructose malabsorption may in some cases be secondary to SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Surgical adhesions
related_conditions.explanation: Abdominal adhesions from prior surgery can create anatomical narrowings or slow transit in the small intestine, creating conditions that favour bacterial overgrowth.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Endometriosis lesions on the bowel, associated adhesions, and neurological changes from pelvic inflammation can impair small intestinal motility, contributing to SIBO. The two conditions are increasingly recognised as commonly co-occurring.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Upper GI symptoms of SIBO — nausea, early satiety, upper abdominal bloating — can overlap significantly with functional dyspepsia. Some cases of functional dyspepsia may have SIBO as a contributing mechanism.

related_conditions.name: H. pylori infection
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori reduces stomach acid production and causes gastric inflammation, which can impair the first line of defence against bacterial overgrowth in the proximal gut. Co-occurrence with SIBO has been noted in research.

related_conditions.name: Parasitic infection
related_conditions.explanation: Parasitic infections (particularly Giardia) can damage intestinal motility and the brush border, creating conditions that allow or worsen SIBO. Post-Giardia SIBO is a recognised clinical pattern.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation / visceral hypersensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic SIBO can sensitise enteric and central pain pathways, leading to visceral hypersensitivity that persists even after the overgrowth is treated. This may explain why some people have ongoing symptoms post-treatment.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: The gut-brain axis is bidirectional — SIBO-related gut inflammation and microbial metabolites may influence anxiety, while anxiety-driven stress hormones can impair gut motility and worsen SIBO.

strategies.name: Antibiotic treatment — rifaximin (with or without neomycin)
strategies.description: Rifaximin is a non-absorbable antibiotic that acts locally in the gut and is the most evidence-based pharmaceutical treatment for hydrogen-dominant SIBO. For methane-dominant SIBO/IMO, combining rifaximin with neomycin or metronidazole may be more effective. A typical course is 10–14 days. Success rates vary widely (40–70%) and relapse is common without addressing underlying drivers.
strategies.notes: Requires a prescription. Many mainstream GPs are unfamiliar with SIBO and may be reluctant to prescribe. Gastroenterologists or functional medicine doctors familiar with SIBO are more likely to offer this. Relapse is the norm if motility issues, dietary patterns, or other drivers are not addressed simultaneously.

strategies.name: Herbal antimicrobial protocol
strategies.description: Herbal protocols using combinations such as oregano oil, berberine, allicin (from garlic), neem, and others have been shown in at least one randomised study to match rifaximin in efficacy for SIBO. Common protocols include the Herbal Antimicrobials protocol studied by Johns Hopkins researchers. Typically administered over 4 weeks.
strategies.notes: May be preferred by those who cannot access rifaximin, are concerned about antibiotic side effects, or have already undergone multiple antibiotic courses. Quality and dosing of herbal preparations vary significantly. Work with a practitioner familiar with these protocols.

strategies.name: Elemental diet
strategies.description: A 2–3 week course of an elemental formula (pre-digested nutrients in liquid form) can starve bacteria in the small intestine by ensuring nearly complete absorption before the formula reaches the distal gut. Research by Dr. Mark Pimentel suggests it achieves breath test normalisation rates of around 80–85%. Used as both a treatment and a diagnostic tool.
strategies.notes: Difficult to sustain — highly restrictive, unpleasant tasting, and expensive. Typically used as a last resort or when antibiotics have repeatedly failed. Semi-elemental formulas may be more tolerable. Requires careful nutritional planning.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP diet (symptom management)
strategies.description: Reducing fermentable carbohydrates (FODMAPs) reduces the substrate available for bacterial fermentation and can significantly reduce bloating, gas, and pain. Widely recommended as a short-to-medium-term symptom management tool alongside SIBO treatment.
strategies.notes: This manages symptoms but does not treat the overgrowth itself. Long-term low-FODMAP eating may actually reduce the diversity of the colonic microbiome. It is intended as a temporary tool, not a permanent dietary identity. A FODMAP-trained dietitian can guide reintroduction once SIBO is treated.

strategies.name: Specific carbohydrate diet (SCD) or SIBO-specific diet
strategies.description: The SCD eliminates complex carbohydrates and grains that feed overgrowth. The SIBO-specific food guide (developed by Dr. Allison Siebecker) combines elements of SCD and low-FODMAP to reduce bacterial feeding substrate while maintaining nutritional adequacy.
strategies.notes: Evidence is largely clinical and experiential rather than from RCTs. Some individuals find significant symptom relief; others find these diets too restrictive to sustain. Best approached with dietitian guidance.

strategies.name: Prokinetic therapy
strategies.description: Prokinetics stimulate gut motility — particularly the migrating motor complex between meals — to prevent bacterial re-accumulation. Options include low-dose naltrexone (LDN), low-dose erythromycin, prucalopride (prescription), iberogast (herbal), ginger, and 5-HTP. Often used as post-treatment maintenance to reduce relapse.
strategies.notes: Arguably one of the most important long-term strategies. Without motility support, SIBO relapse rates are very high. Prescription prokinetics require a doctor. LDN and herbal options may be accessible through integrative or functional medicine practitioners.

strategies.name: Addressing stomach acid adequacy
strategies.description: Adequate stomach acid is a key defence against bacterial overgrowth. Reviewing whether PPI use is necessary, and if appropriate, trialling reduction under medical guidance, may help. Some practitioners assess for hypochlorhydria (low stomach acid) and use betaine HCl supplementation cautiously.
strategies.notes: Do not discontinue PPIs without medical supervision — rebound acid hypersecretion can occur. This is particularly relevant for those on long-term PPIs who develop new GI symptoms.

strategies.name: Targeted nutrient repletion
strategies.description: Replacing deficiencies identified in testing — particularly B12 (often requiring sublingual or injectable forms due to malabsorption), vitamin D, iron, and fat-soluble vitamins. Magnesium supplementation may also help constipation in methane-dominant cases.
strategies.notes: B12 injections or high-dose sublingual B12 bypass intestinal absorption and may be more effective than oral B12 tablets in those with significant malabsorption.

strategies.name: Between-meal fasting intervals
strategies.description: The migrating motor complex (the cleansing wave that sweeps the small intestine) only activates between meals, typically requiring at least 4–5 hours of fasting. Frequent snacking suppresses MMC activity. Extending intervals between eating — including avoiding late-night snacking — can support bacterial clearance.
strategies.notes: This is a low-risk lifestyle modification with a plausible mechanistic rationale. Can be difficult for people with blood sugar regulation issues or eating disorder history — adapt accordingly.

strategies.name: Bismuth subsalicylate (for H2S SIBO)
strategies.description: Bismuth binds hydrogen sulphide in the gut and is sometimes used as part of treatment protocols for H2S SIBO. Available over the counter (e.g. Pepto-Bismol) but used at therapeutic doses in this context.
strategies.notes: Evidence is limited and largely based on clinical practice rather than RCTs. High-dose or prolonged use carries risks including bismuth toxicity. Use only under practitioner guidance.

things_to_avoid: Long-term or indefinite low-FODMAP eating without professional guidance — this manages symptoms but does not treat the cause and may harm colonic microbiome diversity over time

Probiotic supplementation during active SIBO without professional guidance — some evidence suggests probiotics can worsen symptoms and even contribute to brain fog in some SIBO patients by colonising the small intestine
Frequent snacking and grazing — suppresses the migrating motor complex and allows bacteria to re-accumulate between meals
Unnecessary or prolonged PPI use — reduces stomach acid, a key defence against bacterial overgrowth; review necessity with a prescribing doctor
Treating SIBO repeatedly without investigating and addressing the underlying driver — relapse is near-certain without identifying what allowed the overgrowth to occur
High-fibre or prebiotic-heavy diets during active untreated SIBO — fibre feeds bacteria directly and can dramatically worsen symptoms before treatment is completed
Assuming a negative breath test rules out SIBO — these tests have significant false-negative rates, particularly for distal SIBO and H2S SIBO

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Treating SIBO without finding and fixing what caused it will almost always lead to relapse. The bacteria came back because the conditions that allowed them to establish never changed.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The antibiotic course feels like a solution. People want to believe that eradicating the overgrowth is the end of the problem. Investigating motility, stomach acid, structural issues, and underlying conditions is complex, slower, and often requires multiple specialist consultations.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who stay well after SIBO treatment are typically those who identified a specific driver — a motility disorder, PPI overuse, post-infectious MMC damage, hypothyroidism — and addressed it directly, often adding a maintenance prokinetic. They have clear relapse rates that drop over time rather than recurring every few months.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most mainstream gastroenterologists do not offer a thorough SIBO workup or consider prokinetic maintenance. Many people with SIBO receive years of symptomatic IBS management without ever being tested.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients trust that specialists in gut health would investigate gut-based mechanisms. The reality is that SIBO sits at the edge of mainstream gastroenterology, and many practitioners are more familiar with acid suppression, fibre supplementation, and antispasmodics than with breath testing and motility medicine.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who find effective care tend to seek out practitioners who specifically name SIBO in their clinical focus — functional medicine doctors, integrative gastroenterologists, or GPs with an interest in gut health. Asking explicitly whether breath testing is available, and arriving with a documented symptom history, accelerates diagnosis.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A low-FODMAP diet controls SIBO symptoms very effectively, which can mask the condition and delay treatment for years. Feeling better on a restricted diet is not the same as being well.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Symptom control feels like success. Restrictive diets create a sense of control. The idea that the diet is masking an unresolved problem rather than solving it is unsettling, and re-expanding the diet to test the underlying state is uncomfortable.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who move through this tend to use the low-FODMAP diet as a diagnostic and short-term management tool — consciously, with an end date — while pursuing breath testing and investigating underlying drivers. The goal becomes treating the cause so that dietary restriction can be reduced, not permanent limitation.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: SIBO symptoms — brain fog, fatigue, bloating — can persist after successful bacterial eradication, sometimes for months. This is not failure of treatment; it is the aftermath of intestinal inflammation and sensitisation that takes time to resolve.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The expectation is that a successful antibiotic course should produce rapid, dramatic improvement. When symptoms persist, people often conclude that treatment failed and pursue repeated antibiotic courses, which may not be necessary or helpful.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Practitioners experienced in SIBO typically counsel patients that post-treatment healing — repair of the intestinal lining, desensitisation of a sensitised enteric nervous system, rebalancing of the colonic microbiome — takes months, not weeks. People who understand this timeline avoid overtreatment and support recovery with gut-healing approaches rather than repeated antimicrobials.

key_levers.lever: Identify and address the underlying driver of SIBO
key_levers.why_it_matters: Without fixing the condition that allowed bacteria to accumulate — impaired MMC, hypochlorhydria, structural issue, thyroid dysfunction — bacteria will re-establish. This is the single biggest predictor of long-term success.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a practitioner to systematically review potential drivers: history of food poisoning (post-infectious SIBO), PPI use, motility symptoms, connective tissue disorder, hypothyroidism, prior surgery. Consider anti-CdtB/anti-vinculin testing if post-infectious pattern is suspected.
key_levers.timeline: Investigation may take 1–3 months; addressing the driver may take longer depending on the cause

key_levers.lever: Complete a full antimicrobial course targeted to the correct subtype
key_levers.why_it_matters: Partially treating or treating the wrong subtype (e.g. using rifaximin alone for methane-dominant SIBO) produces incomplete eradication, symptom relapse, and potentially more resistant bacterial populations.
key_levers.how_to_start: Obtain a breath test that measures both hydrogen AND methane (and ideally H2S). Share results with a SIBO-experienced practitioner who can select the appropriate protocol.
key_levers.timeline: Treatment course is typically 10–14 days for antibiotics; 4 weeks for herbal protocols. Repeat breath testing 2–4 weeks after treatment to assess response.

key_levers.why_it_matters: The MMC is the mechanism that keeps the small intestine clear between meals. In many SIBO patients it is impaired. Prokinetics stimulate this mechanism and are one of the most effective tools for reducing relapse.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss options with a practitioner: low-dose naltrexone, low-dose erythromycin, prucalopride, iberogast, ginger, or 5-HTP. Even extending meal spacing to 4–5 hour intervals stimulates natural MMC activity.
key_levers.timeline: Prokinetics are typically maintained for 3–12 months post-treatment

key_levers.lever: Extend fasting intervals between meals and stop late-night eating
key_levers.why_it_matters: The MMC only operates in a fasted state. Constant eating — particularly snacking or grazing — suppresses the cleansing wave entirely. This is a modifiable lifestyle factor that anyone can begin immediately.
key_levers.how_to_start: Aim for minimum 4-hour gaps between eating episodes. Stop eating at least 2–3 hours before sleep. This is a low-cost, evidence-aligned change that begins immediately.
key_levers.timeline: This is an ongoing maintenance practice, not a time-limited intervention

key_levers.lever: Repair the gut lining after treatment
key_levers.why_it_matters: SIBO-related inflammation and bacterial products can damage intestinal permeability and brush border enzymes. Supporting mucosal healing may reduce food sensitivities and improve overall absorption.
key_levers.how_to_start: Commonly recommended approaches include zinc carnosine, L-glutamine, collagen/bone broth, omega-3 fatty acids, and reducing ultra-processed food intake. These are best implemented after active treatment, not during.
key_levers.timeline: Mucosal healing is generally considered a 3–6 month process

key_levers.lever: Work with a FODMAP-trained dietitian for structured reintroduction after treatment
key_levers.why_it_matters: Many secondary food intolerances — lactose, fructose, some FODMAPs — may be driven by SIBO rather than being intrinsic to the person. Structured reintroduction after treatment reveals which restrictions are truly necessary, reducing unnecessary long-term dietary limitation.
key_levers.how_to_start: Engage a registered dietitian with FODMAP training (ideally with GI specialisation) after completing antimicrobial treatment and allowing 4–6 weeks for initial recovery.
key_levers.timeline: The FODMAP reintroduction process typically takes 8–12 weeks

common_mistakes: Doing repeated antibiotic courses without investigating why SIBO keeps coming back — this is the most common cycle people get stuck in

Staying on a highly restrictive SIBO diet indefinitely rather than treating the underlying condition — this leads to nutritional deficiency and reduced quality of life without resolving the root problem
Taking probiotics during active SIBO — some research (Pimentel et al.) links probiotic use in SIBO to worsened brain fog and slower symptom resolution; timing and strain selection matter
Interpreting a negative breath test as definitively ruling out SIBO — false negatives are well-documented, particularly for distal SIBO and H2S subtype
Treating SIBO aggressively while ignoring co-existing motility conditions, thyroid issues, or connective tissue disorders — these upstream causes ensure ongoing relapse
Using high-fibre, high-prebiotic foods as a health strategy during active untreated SIBO — feeding bacteria that are in the wrong place dramatically worsens symptoms
Assuming that symptom improvement on a restricted diet means the SIBO is resolved — symptom control is not eradication

specialists.role: Can order and interpret breath testing, prescribe rifaximin and combination antibiotic protocols, and investigate structural or motility-related drivers. The most important caveat is that many general gastroenterologists have limited familiarity with SIBO beyond basic breath testing.
specialists.notes: Seek a gastroenterologist who explicitly mentions SIBO in their clinical focus. Academic and integrative GI practices are more likely to have practitioners experienced in SIBO management and prokinetic therapy.

specialists.role: Likely to take a comprehensive root-cause approach — investigating motility, stomach acid, the post-infectious mechanism, thyroid function, and co-existing conditions. More likely to offer herbal protocols, prokinetics, and gut-healing strategies alongside or instead of antibiotics.
specialists.notes: Quality and training vary. Look for practitioners certified through the Institute for Functional Medicine (IFM) or with documented clinical experience in gut health and SIBO specifically.

specialists.role: Essential for guiding dietary management during and after treatment — including structured FODMAP reintroduction, nutritional adequacy during restriction phases, and identifying which food intolerances are truly primary versus secondary to SIBO.
specialists.notes: Look for RDs who are Monash University FODMAP-certified or who list SIBO or gut health as a clinical specialisation. Mainstream dietitians may not be familiar with the nuances of SIBO dietary management.

specialists.type: Integrative or naturopathic doctor (ND) with GI focus
specialists.role: May offer herbal antimicrobial protocols, comprehensive stool testing, nutritional deficiency assessment, and a longer consultation model that allows for systematic investigation. Can be useful where access to functional medicine doctors is limited.
specialists.notes: Regulation and training standards for NDs vary significantly by country and state. In regions where NDs are regulated healthcare professionals (e.g. Canada, several US states), they can offer a structured clinical approach.

specialists.type: Endocrinologist or GP with thyroid expertise
specialists.role: If hypothyroidism is suspected as a driver of impaired motility and SIBO, optimising thyroid function is a critical part of SIBO management. An endocrinologist or GP with thyroid expertise is the appropriate practitioner for this component.
specialists.notes: Thyroid screening should include TSH, free T3, and free T4 at minimum. Some integrative practitioners also assess reverse T3 and thyroid antibodies.

patient_communities.name: r/SIBO
patient_communities.notes: One of the largest and most active SIBO communities online. Extensive searchable archive of treatment experiences, breath test interpretation questions, protocol discussions, and practitioner recommendations. Quality varies — critically evaluate advice against clinical sources.

patient_communities.name: SIBO SOS Community
patient_communities.notes: Founded by Shivan Sarna, a SIBO patient-advocate. Hosts interviews with leading SIBO researchers and clinicians. The associated Facebook group is large and active. Good resource for staying updated on evolving SIBO research.

patient_communities.name: The IBS & SIBO Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Large Facebook group covering IBS and SIBO overlap. Useful for peer experience sharing, though moderation quality can vary.

patient_communities.name: SIBO Patient Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Patient-run Facebook community with active discussion of treatment protocols, testing, and practitioner experiences.

learning_resources.title: SIBOInfo.com — Dr. Allison Siebecker's SIBO resource
learning_resources.description: The most comprehensive free online resource on SIBO from one of the field's leading clinical educators. Covers causes, testing, dietary approaches, treatment protocols, and the SIBO-specific food guide. Regularly updated and clinically grounded. An essential starting point for self-directed investigation.

learning_resources.title: The Microbiome Connection: Your Guide to IBS, SIBO, and Low-Fermentation Eating
learning_resources.author_or_source: Dr. Mark Pimentel and Dr. Ali Rezaie
learning_resources.description: Written by the researchers behind much of the foundational SIBO science, including the post-infectious model and the development of rifaximin protocols. Explains the science in accessible terms and provides practical guidance on the low-fermentation eating approach. Highly credible — these are the researchers who published the pivotal SIBO-IBS studies.

learning_resources.title: Fast Tract Digestion: IBS — Heartburn, Constipation, Bloating, Acid Reflux Linked to Bacterial Overgrowth
learning_resources.description: Introduces the Fast Tract Diet, an alternative dietary approach to SIBO management based on the fermentation potential of foods rather than FODMAP categories. Offers a useful complementary framework to low-FODMAP eating, with a strong mechanistic rationale.

learning_resources.title: SIBO SOS Summits — expert interview series
learning_resources.description: A series of recorded interviews with leading SIBO researchers and clinicians including Dr. Pimentel, Dr. Siebecker, Dr. Steven Sandberg-Lewis, and others. Covers testing, treatment protocols, prokinetics, diet, and co-occurring conditions. Accessible to non-specialists and a good way to hear directly from experts in the field.

learning_resources.title: Pimentel et al. (2011) — A Link Between Irritable Bowel Syndrome and Fibromyalgia May Be Related to Findings on Lactulose Breath Testing
learning_resources.description: One of several foundational papers by Pimentel's group establishing the SIBO-IBS link, the post-infectious model, and the role of antibodies against CdtB and vinculin. For those who want to understand the research basis of the post-infectious SIBO model, searching PubMed for Pimentel SIBO provides access to the key primary literature.

learning_resources.title: The Gutbliss Podcast / Robynne Chutkan
learning_resources.description: A gastroenterologist's perspective on gut health, microbiome science, and conditions including SIBO. Accessible, clinically grounded, and balanced between mainstream gastroenterology and the emerging functional understanding of gut disorders.

learning_resources.title: Elemental Diet for SIBO: research and protocol overview
learning_resources.description: A detailed overview of the elemental diet as a SIBO treatment, including the original research, protocol specifics, and practical guidance. Essential reading for anyone considering this intensive dietary intervention.

sections.relevance_signal: Bloating, gas, and digestive symptoms that don't resolve despite dietary changes — especially if symptoms worsen after eating fermentable foods, carbohydrates, or fibre, and if there is a history of food poisoning, antibiotic use, or a diagnosis of IBS that hasn't improved with standard treatment.
